text: This chapter describes how the **TesTly** platform was tested and evaluated against its functional requirements and quality goals. Because TesTly is a multi-platform system — a NestJS backend, a Next.js web application, and a Flutter mobile application sharing one API contract — a single testing technique is insufficient. The project therefore adopts a **layer-appropriate testing strategy** in which each application is verified with the framework idiomatic to its stack, supported by a rich seeded demonstration dataset that allows realistic, repeatable quality-assurance (QA) walkthroughs of every role. The chapter then evaluates the user experience of the delivered system, summarises the advantages it offers over the status quo, and honestly documents the limitations that remain at the conclusion of the graduation project.
Functional Testing

Functional testing confirms that each feature of TesTly behaves as specified across the three applications. The testing approach is organised per layer: the backend is covered by automated **unit and integration tests** and an **end-to-end (e2e) smoke harness**; the web frontend is covered by **end-to-end user-journey tests**; and the mobile application carries the broadest **widget and unit test** suite of the three. These automated suites are complemented by manual QA walkthroughs performed against a deterministic seeded dataset, following the project's deployment checklist.
Backend Testing — Jest Unit and End-to-End Tests

The backend is tested with **Jest** (using `ts-jest`), configured inline in `apps/backend/package.json` with a `testRegex` of `.*\.spec\.ts$`, a `src` root directory, and a Node test environment. There are **18 colocated `.spec.ts` files** that cover the critical surface of the system: the authentication service and controller, the bookings service and controller, the patient service and controller, the lab service and controller, the lab-storage service, the admin service and controller, the public-labs controller, the FAQ service and controller, the notifications service and controller, and the Prisma service. These tests exercise the business rules that protect health data and money — for example, the payment-before-confirmation gate, the one-step home-test-kit state machine, and the lab-activation readiness checks.

End-to-end tests use **Jest together with Supertest** against a fully bootstrapped Nest application, configured separately through `test/jest-e2e.json` (`testRegex` of `.e2e-spec.ts$`) and executed with `npm run test:e2e`. The current e2e harness (`test/app.e2e-spec.ts`) boots the complete `AppModule` and asserts the root endpoint, acting as a smoke test that proves the entire dependency-injection graph wires up correctly and the HTTP server responds. Coverage can be collected with `npm run test:cov`, configured via `collectCoverageFrom: ["**/*.(t|j)s"]`.
Web Frontend Testing — Playwright User Journeys

The Next.js web application is tested at the end-to-end level with **Playwright** (`apps/frontend/playwright.config.ts`), structured around the three user roles. There are exactly three specification files — `patient-journey.spec.ts`, `lab-journey.spec.ts`, and `admin-journey.spec.ts`. The configuration is production-aware: it runs against Chromium, auto-starts the development server as its `webServer` (reusing a running instance locally), and, under continuous-integration conditions, enables `forbidOnly`, two retries, a single worker, and trace capture on first retry for debuggability. The patient journey, for example, exercises the guest-search experience, then login, then viewing seeded results — validating that the application functions correctly against the seeded demonstration data.
Mobile Testing — Flutter Widget and Unit Tests

The Flutter application carries the most extensive automated test suite in the project — roughly **20 Dart test files** under `apps/mobile/test/`, organised to mirror the feature-first `lib/` architecture. Coverage spans the networking layer (`api_exception_test.dart`), secure token storage, the notification service and repository, and — most thoroughly — the patient feature set: authentication (models, repository, session notifier, and the login screen widget), labs, the booking flow notifier and repository, the workspace aggregation, and the health-profile trends. Tests use `flutter_test` with **`mocktail`** for mocking, exploiting Riverpod's provider-override mechanism to inject fakes in place of real repositories, and are executed with `flutter test`.
Seeded Demo Accounts and QA Scenarios

To make functional testing realistic and repeatable, the project ships a rich seeded demonstration dataset, populated by `npm run db:seed`. The seed creates **one admin, 21 active labs, additional pending and rejected lab accounts, and several patients with historical results**, so that cross-lab health-trend tracking and the admin governance views have meaningful data on first run. For convenience during QA, all demonstration accounts share the password `password123`; on the production web login screen the demo-credential hints are hidden by setting `NEXT_PUBLIC_SHOW_DEMO_CREDENTIALS` to `false`.

QA scenarios follow the manual gate prescribed in `docs/DEPLOYMENT_CHECKLIST.md`, which walks a smoke flow for every role: a guest browses labs and tests without registering; a patient registers, books, completes a (simulated) payment, views the result PDF, and submits a review; and lab staff confirm a booking, upload a result, and mark it delivered. Operational checks confirm that audit events are emitted, that the uploads directory is writable under the local storage driver, and that pagination limits bound responses.

Table 5.1 lists a representative subset of the functional test cases executed across the three applications. Each maps to behaviour verified either by an automated suite or by the seeded-data QA walkthrough, and each is reported with a final status of **Pass**.

Clear "Wrong account type" error returned, not a generic invalid-credentials message

Used token revoked, a fresh access/refresh pair issued; reused old token rejected

Booking created as Pending with a +100 EGP surcharge and the home address recorded

Upload rejected by MIME check and %PDF magic-byte validation

text: User Experience Testing

Beyond verifying that features work, the project evaluated whether they are pleasant and accessible to use. User-experience (UX) testing focused on usability, responsive and adaptive design, dark-mode support, accessibility, and navigation, and was conducted through guided demonstration walkthroughs of the patient, lab, and admin journeys against the seeded dataset.
Usability and Visual Consistency

Both clients present a consistent visual language drawn from a shared design system. The web application expresses its design tokens entirely in CSS through Tailwind 4 — a blue brand accent (`--brand #2563eb`), a neutral surface palette, semantic success/warning/destructive colours, and a chart palette — and the Flutter application ports the same palette from `globals.css` into Material 3 themes built with `ColorScheme.fromSeed`. The result is a uniform look across roles and platforms: white rounded cards on a light-grey background, pill-style tabs, and `lucide-react` icons on the web mirrored by equivalent iconography on mobile.

Usability is reinforced by consistent feedback scaffolding. The web app provides a single toast system (`useToast().success/error/info`) that drives every dashboard mutation, a shared breadcrumb component, and a single shimmering `.skeleton` utility used for all loading states. Errors are handled uniformly through a typed `ApiError`: `401` redirects to login, `403` to an access-denied screen, and `404`/`409` surface specialised messages. The mobile app mirrors this with reusable `EmptyState`, `ErrorState`, and skeleton widgets, and a motion toolkit (`FadeSlideIn`, `CountUpText`, `AnimatedProgressBar`) that honours the operating-system "reduce motion" setting.
Responsive and Adaptive Design

The web application is built mobile-first with Tailwind utility classes and adapts fluidly from phone to desktop widths — for example, the lab-search experience renders a sticky filter sidebar alongside a results column on wide screens. The Flutter application is natively adaptive, using shell routes with bottom navigation for both the patient and lab roles, collapsing app bars (`SliverAppBar`) on detail screens, and horizontally scrolling carousels for featured labs. Geolocation is integrated on both clients so that distance-based sorting adapts to the user's position, with the mobile and web `haversineKm` helpers computing identical distances.
Dark Mode

The mobile application provides first-class dark-mode support: `AppTheme` defines complete light and dark Material 3 themes, and a `ThemeController` lets the user choose System, Light, or Dark from the profile screen, defaulting to `ThemeMode.system` and persisting the choice. On the web, a `dark` custom variant is declared in the stylesheet and the error and loading boundaries are dark-mode aware, although the web app ships light by default.
Accessibility and Navigation

Accessibility considerations are present throughout. The web breadcrumb is a semantic `<nav aria-label="Breadcrumb">`, forms use real `<label>` elements, and the password field offers a show/hide toggle. The clinically aware charts colour abnormal readings red and in-range readings green and pair every chart with a textual point-by-point breakdown, so trend information is not conveyed by colour alone. Navigation is predictable: the web uses Next.js App Router with edge middleware that routes each role to its correct dashboard, while the mobile app uses `go_router` with a central role-aware redirect that sends patients, lab staff, and admins (the last to a "use the web app" screen) to the appropriate destination. The mobile application additionally ships full **English and Arabic localization** with right-to-left support, each ARB file carrying 330 keys.
Demonstration Walkthroughs
Three guided walkthroughs were used to evaluate each role's journey end to end against the seeded data.

items: Patient journey: from the public landing page the patient searches for a test, compares labs by price, rating, and distance, opens a lab's detail page, books an appointment (lab visit, home collection, or home test kit), completes a simulated online payment or selects a cash option, tracks the booking, views the delivered result as a PDF and structured summary, inspects cross-lab health trends, submits a review, and consults the agentic AI health assistant.

Lab journey: lab staff sign in to the workspace, work the booking queue (confirming or rejecting requests, recording cash receipts, advancing home-test-kit fulfilment), manage the test catalogue and schedule slots, upload official result PDFs with structured panels, mark results delivered, review analytics (revenue estimate, capacity usage, top tests), and open the privacy-scoped patient-context drawer.

Admin journey: the administrator reviews the platform dashboard, filters labs by onboarding status, expands a lab to inspect its onboarding-readiness summary, approves a ready lab or rejects an incomplete one, browses patients and recent payments, and reads the analytics charts for booking volume, revenue by city, and popular tests.

caption: Patient journey evaluated during the user-experience walkthroughs

text: Advantages and Benefits of the System

Evaluated against the fragmented status quo of laboratory testing in Egypt — where patients phone individual labs, cannot compare prices, and collect paper results that no system aggregates — TesTly delivers several concrete advantages.

items: **Unified marketplace.** TesTly brings many accredited labs into a single, searchable storefront, letting patients discover and compare tests by price, rating, distance, accreditation, and turnaround time. The same test offered by multiple labs is grouped into one comparison view that surfaces the cheapest price and the number of labs offering it.

**Transparency.** Prices, accreditations, turnaround times, and published patient reviews are visible before booking. Reviewer names are privacy-reduced to a "First L." form, and a lab's denormalized rating is recomputed from published reviews so listings stay both fast and trustworthy.

**Multi-channel booking.** Patients can book a lab visit, a home sample collection, or a home test kit, each with its own scheduling, surcharge, and fulfilment logic — including a strict four-stage kit-tracking state machine — so the platform matches how people actually want to be served.

**Flexible payment.** Online (simulated gateway) and three cash methods are supported, with the real-world distinction between prepaid online orders and pay-on-service cash orders enforced at confirmation, and refunds modelled as an explicit status transition.

**Structured results and cross-lab trends.** Results are stored in two complementary forms — an authoritative PDF and normalized structured observations. A clinical-normalization layer maps differently named analytes to canonical markers and converts units (for example glucose mmol/L to mg/dL, HbA1c IFCC to NGSP), enabling safe health-trend tracking even across labs that report in different units.

**AI guidance.** An agentic AI assistant, powered by Google Gemini, answers patient questions and can call real catalog tools (`find_labs`, `search_tests`) to surface clickable lab and test cards grounded in live data, never inventing prices or availability, while a lightweight guided-FAQ chatbot provides a deterministic, quota-free fallback on every page.

**Multi-platform reach.** A Next.js web application and a native Flutter iOS/Android application share one backend, giving patients and lab staff a consistent experience on whichever device they prefer; the mobile app adds biometric unlock, push notifications, and offline-secure token storage.

**Security and privacy by design.** Health data is protected with bcrypt password hashing, rotating SHA-256-hashed refresh tokens, layered role-based access control, ownership-checked authenticated PDF downloads with magic-byte validation, and a patient-controlled lab-history sharing setting that governs exactly what each lab may see.

text: Limitations of the System

As a graduation project delivered within a fixed timeframe, TesTly has a number of honest limitations. Documenting them transparently both scopes the present work and frames the future work discussed in the next chapter.

items: **Payment gateway is a simulation.** Online payment is implemented as a faithful demo (`demo-online-payment`) that mirrors what a real gateway webhook would do — setting a synthetic reference and the appropriate status — but no real charge is made and no live PayMob integration is wired in. The design localises this so a real provider could be dropped in later.

**Manual structured-result entry.** Structured observations that power cross-lab trending must be entered by lab staff alongside the official PDF; there is no automatic extraction of values from the uploaded document, so trend data depends on labs taking that extra step.

**Coverage limited to seeded labs.** The live catalogue consists of the seeded demonstration labs (21 active labs and supporting accounts) rather than real, contracted laboratories; the platform has not yet been populated with production lab partners.

**Push notifications constrained on simulators.** Firebase Cloud Messaging push is fully wired but degrades gracefully when Firebase credentials are absent, and reliable push delivery requires real devices and APNS configuration, limiting what can be demonstrated on emulators and simulators.
**No Arabic web UI yet.** While the mobile application ships full English/Arabic localization with RTL, the web application is currently English-only; Arabic web localization remains future work.
**Google Maps not yet rendered.** Location and distance are surfaced numerically; the Google Maps plugin and API key are provisioned on mobile but no interactive map view is rendered yet.

**No automated CI gate.** Although substantial test suites exist for all three apps, there is no GitHub Actions pipeline enforcing them before deploy; quality is currently governed by a manual deployment checklist rather than an automated machine gate.
**Admin console is web-only.** By design the administrative functions are available only on the web; the mobile app redirects admin users to a "use the web app" screen.

**Operational hardening pending.** A live developer `.env` file containing real credentials in the working tree highlights a secret-hygiene gap, and rate limiting and more comprehensive monitoring are not yet in place — appropriate next steps before any real-world deployment handling medical data.
